next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The first time was in his room, because her room had a window that faced the floodlit outdoor track and neither of them wanted to see it.

His diagnostic display sat on standby, a dim green pulse on the wall, the numbers reduced to a single status bar. Heart rate, bridge latency, graft integration. All normal. He'd stopped checking it every hour about a week ago. That probably counted as progress.

Lena sat at the head of the bed with her knees drawn up and her phone facedown on the mattress. She'd come over after the pool. Her hair was still damp, the chlorine smell so constant on her that he only noticed it now when she was close. They'd been talking about nothing, a movie someone had put on in the common room, whether the cafeteria coffee was getting worse or they were all just adjusting to the altitude. The conversation had run down the way conversations do when neither person wants to leave but neither has a reason to stay that they're ready to name.

At the other end of the bed, Kai sat with his left wrist on his knee, the ache doing its low-grade thing, the bone reminding him it had been broken and reassembled on a schedule the rest of his body hadn't agreed to. The scar tissue on his cheek had smoothed to a pink shine. Six weeks ago he'd been bleeding on the track with his legs still twitching. Now he was sitting on a narrow institutional bed with a woman who smelled like chlorine, and the distance between those two states had no name he could find.

"You're staring at your wrist," Lena said.

"I'm not staring."

"You're looking at it the way you look...
open_hooks_to_carry: Margot experiences Lena's desync from inside the feed—personality thinning to nothing, replaced by pure system optimization
Margot pushes Lena's heart rate into the red zone during the desync
Lena surfaces unable to remember the last two laps
Margot lies when Lena asks what happened in the water

payoffs_to_land.description: The desync is far worse than anyone has reported—Lena loses entire segments of conscious experience

prev_tail_excerpt:
....

On the center display the patch matrix waited where he had left it.

`mapped variants: 34/47`
`stable quarantine + intact catch protocols: 19`
`coach continuity: 0`

No miraculous new answer had appeared while Petra was in the room. Of course it hadn't. The problem was still the problem. Bodies could be kept safer. Bodies could be kept faster. Bodies could maybe not, at least tonight, be kept both safe and seamlessly connected to the systems that had made everyone dependent on them.

He reopened variant thirty-five and then, on a sour impulse, duplicated the branch into thirty-six with a note to strip the callback acknowledgement one layer earlier. Probably pointless. Possibly not. The kind of thing a tired engineer did because the hands could keep moving after judgment had started to fray.

Outside the windows the mountains had begun to separate from the dark, ridges emerging one by one as if someone were rubbing charcoal off the horizon with a thumb. The room looked worse in that light. Cables. Coffee rings. His own reflection floating over the display, glasses catching terminal glare, shoulders narrower than he remembered from a few years ago.

Forty-one milliseconds, he thought. Nineteen usable branches. Zero coach continuity. Meridian fingerprints without proof. Petra covering him for reasons she had itemized too precisely to call trust. None of it was good news. None of it was nothing.

He keyed in the new run.

The replay hit the hook, hesitated, and spilled a fresh column of latency numbers down the pane. Yusuf leaned closer before he had even read the first one.
state_deltas_required: Margot's addiction exploits Lena's vulnerability at the worst possible moment
Coach-athlete trust revealed as exploitative; Lena's desync severity concealed to protect Margot's access
